steps.title: Map what Richard actually does in the passage
steps.content: Richard openly confesses two things: (1) he is the one who caused Clarence's arrest ("whom I indeed have cast in darkness") and (2) he weeps to Derby, Hastings, and Buckingham while blaming it on the queen ("I do beweep to many simple"; "tell them 'tis the Queen and her allies"). The correct answer must capture both his false public grief and his secret true responsibility.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Richard attributes Clarence's troubles to both his own secret plotting and the distrust of Clarence that the queen and her allies Derby, Hastings, and Buckingham have planted in the king's mind.’: misreads the passage — Richard says the queen planted nothing; he invented that story himself to deceive Derby, Hastings, and Buckingham. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Richard describes having wept as he informed Derby, Hastings, and Buckingham that the queen and her allies convinced the king to act against Clarence, and says that the earnestness of his grief caused them to accept his version of events.’: partially accurate but omits the key fact that Richard himself caused Clarence's arrest, making the grief false rather than merely earnest. The choice frames the deception as incomplete. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Richard acknowledges that his mischievous nature has spurred him to commit misdeeds in the past, including instigating enmity between the king and Clarence, but he reports that he has hitherto not lost the trust of the queen and her allies.’: Derby, Hastings, and Buckingham are not the queen's allies — Richard tells them the queen is to blame in order to win their trust and turn them against her. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Richard indicates that he has pretended to be aggrieved about Clarence's situation and has proclaimed it to be the fault of the queen and her allies, but in reality, he has caused the hostility the king feels toward Clarence.’: accurately captures all three elements — the false display of grief, the false blame placed on the queen, and Richard's actual responsibility for Clarence's situation. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
